item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well this was unsettling. as a parent, I felt keenly uncomfortable. I'd love to know more… how these things came into existence and such.
but I still enjoyed it. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from the now deleted Flickr page of Pink Sherbet Photography. With no indication the image is still available under Creative Commons, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but it really feels like it needs more substance. A no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what would happen if:
The parents also had SCP-053?
The parents were under the control of SCP-597?
SCP-352 found a few instances?
Scp-1810 decided to take care of one?
SCP-573 is played for them?
You put a bunch of them that had bonded with parents into 682 or 096 or 058 cell/cube/pen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent. Also, would like to kick whoever made this species/constructs until they can't eat without a straw.

… yes, they seem artificial. I mean, how do they reproduce? Tim Scriven's comment, right above this one, is fairly interesting, but there is no indication of such a thing happening once the neglected children are recovered and the instance of SCP-1076 is dragged into containment. As far as we know, there is no reproductive process, and they do not need to feed.

They are not predators. They are poison. A terror weapon, or more precisely, a torture device to deplete populations as fast as possible; that, or a really misbehaving aberration of evolution (plus anomaly).

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: seconded, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Once established in a home, SCP-1076 begins to monopolize the attention of the parents to the exclusion of their own children. Children in the home begin to show signs of neglect and malnutrition and become depressed, sullen, and withdrawn."

I think I've figured out how SCP-1076 propagates itself.

item.title: wow
item.content:encoded: They keep it real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt this was going in an interesting direction or even SCP-izing feral children, but two major things caused me to downvote:

1) Not enough was done with it to distinguish it from other "subtle predator" type SCPs

2) The compulsion thing is too homogenous. Basically treating everyone exposed to it exactly the same. What if it encounters a parent suffering post-partem depression? What if it encounters a sociopath? What if a boy-1076 encounters a mother with only daughters who hates boys? What happens if it encounters a pedophile?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Oh gods, this one. I count this among the skips that "sold" the site for me and convinced me to sign up.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: +101. Hadn't read this one, definitely like it. Got to wonder how exact their original containment went down and if it's worth it to keep multiple of these things locked up. Also, seems like they could be locked in a box and ignored indefinitely.
Glad to put this at +100. It's their behavior towards each other that sells it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the opposite idea from the vivisection mention; it seems almost like some of the Foundation personnel are unconsciously letting the anomalous effects get to them and won't let the poor widdle monstrous parasitic family killing abominations come to harm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article could be improved if it had a little more information on the SCP's feeding habits and how it selects victims. Aside from that? Brilliiant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They look pathetic, but that's camouflage. They fight because they (rightly) see each other as competition. And apparently they feed on discord, misery and death (of those who would nurture them, no less).

These are not cuckoos. Cuckoos are brood parasites.

These are apex predators.

Yikes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded, especially with regards to the first point. This is exactly what I was expecting as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted simply because the first footnote really creeped me out. "Yeah, they look pathetic, but you can't help them, so why even try?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am angry with you for introducing me to this series, because I'm behind on enough things as it is. It's not your fault, but I'm annoyed anyway.

(Thanks, though. It is interesting.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I kept expecting something about the neglected children converting to new specimens, maybe only rarely or when certain specific criteria are met. They all hate each other because they recognize "one of those things" that caused their parents to forget about them and that they blame for their current misery. It just seemed like the obvious extension, and a solid reason for why this thing targets parents with children below a certain age.

Second, 21 seems like a really high age cutoff. I think the vast majority of 17+ year olds (and a decreasing percentage as you go to younger teenagers) would be able to handle themselves, no matter how their parents were self-destructing over their new pet. Granted, they would probably be really bitter over the whole abondonment thing, but they aren't likely to not wash themselves to the point of sickness or allow themselves to go hungry for very long.

Third, there really needs to be something in the containment about how to detect these things. Monitoring for disruptions in the foster system, or tracking police reports for statistical clusters of child neglect cases, for example.

Final opinion, I really like the idea and it's solidly written, so no down-vote; but I just don't like it enough to up-vote yet.

item.title: Photo Source
item.content:encoded: I got the photo from here. It's under the Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ditched the incident report for now. Couldn't whip it into a shape I was happy with, may revisit later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, replaced "black water" with "several inches of dirty water". Open to any other suggestions about the incident report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new incident log needs work to be up to par with the rest of the article. "Black water?" Really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…the lack of cohesiveness is still bugging me, and also the line about anyone who watched two kids fight each other to death probably needing psychological looking-after (does that really need to be stated? it almost tips into into humor territory…)

But as I considered downvoting I realized that I was distracted by that kid's eyes, in an allured-but-offput kind of way. For that alone? Nicely picked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The competition between specimens is what secured my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's also the likelihood that people with children already have certain psychological and hormonal levels that are perfect for exploitation that even someone "desperately wanting" a child wouldn't have. As there's no clear cut explanation as to HOW the SCP is able to monopolize peoples' attentions, it's quite possible that there's a pheromone or similar thing going on that current-parents are more susceptible to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing that occurred to me after reading.

I see the logic in making the people who take in this SCP already being parents, certainly. It works well. But I think you've missed an area for exploration: It seems to me that it would be just as, if not more likely to affect people who don't, or especially those who can'thave kids but desperately want them. The desire to reproduce can do funny things to peoples' heads, I've observed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An infestation typically ends with the deaths of the parents and other caretakers as they neglect their own well-being in order to attend to SCP-1076, eventually succumbing to starvation or disease.

What happens then? Does the SCP simply move onto more victims? Does it grow into the adult phase of its life-cycle? Maybe it eats the corpses of the caretakers?

Upon investigating, these authorities become obsessed with the welfare of SCP-1076 and subsequently ignore the children.

Seems like this could cause an infestation to rapidly spiral into something needing a lot of cover up: when the civilians are no longer able to care for the SCP the social worker takes it to an orphanage. The adults at the orphanage all obsess over the SCP, leaving all the other orphans unfed and unsupervised. Other orphans all get into a lot of trouble, drawing attention.

Alternatively: social worker #1 investigates, becomes obsessed with SCP, drops all other cases due to obsession. Social worker #2 wonders what's up with that, investigates, becomes obsessed; lather, rinse, repeat. Soon the entire social services office of the city has ceased to function, drawing attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unrelated but fun fact: if the cuckoo's foster father gets rid of the freeloader, the cuckoo's real parents come back and kill him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the subtle suggestion that the Foundation is becoming affected by this SCP. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made the cell less cliché and more claustrophobic. Just chuck these things in a closet. Got rid of "exploratory".

Thinking about adding an interview log with one of the surviving parents to give more detail on what this thing does, but the basic idea is that it's insatiable and unsatisfiable - if you get it clean it will become filthy and bedraggled in no time, and nothing can make it happy.

Vivisection request is there to show that the researchers don't think of these things as even vaguely human, and it was only denied because it was a bit premature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was, but it appears to have been deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect the premise is that the projected "need to protect them" keeps them from being vivisected, in spite of the fact the Foundation has "spares".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You do know what the C stands for, yes? You know, CONTAIN? As in as much of the SCP as possible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the cuckoo analogy as well. I love that and the territoriality, which suggests that these things aren't just modified humans but something entirely separate. I think the tone just gets me in this, it feels very classic SCP: referring to them as 'infestations', denying them food because they don't actually need it. Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am generally neutral on this. Minor issues: 3x3x3m cell (another 10' cube) , They appear biological but don't eat? The Foundation would study that. Why use the term 'exploratory vivisection'? What happens if you bathe them and dress them in clean clothes?

It sort of feels like those commercials where one of these kids would compel you to send money, "Just 47 cents a day will assuage your guilt."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is in dire need of an incident report. Also, are these things just showing up? If so, should there be some sort of direction for how to detect them (monitoring DCPS maybe)? Seems like there would never need be more than one in SCP custody at a time since they kill each other on site (heck, do we need more than one to study?). Neutral for now but I'll be watching edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool, but it just doesn`t for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I seem to remember that there is already is a creepypasta somewhere on the site which is based on the reproductive tendencies of cuckoos, although I can't seem to find it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I love the idea of a cuckoo-like SCP— it seems like a thing that needs to be. But this is one of those cases where all I can think about is the chapter of that favorite work of fiction that does it so much better.

Neutral for now, but I really do love the idea. Maybe… if it skimped a little on description, and described an incident? Actually, I'd almost rather see this as a Tale. But that is just my taste talking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cuckoo birds reproduce by laying their eggs in the nest of other birds. When it hatches, the cuckoo chick pushes the other eggs out of the nest and is fed by the owner of the nest it invaded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is "The Cuckoo?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. An interesting take on The Cuckoo, but it doesn't snag me. Downvote.
